judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently identify the same root causes and fixes with comparable depth and accuracy; Report 1 adds slightly more historical git-commit sourcing while Report 2 adds a regression-test suggestion, but neither is substantively stronger against the expectations.

report_1.evidence: In Postgres, the table owner is exempt from a table's row-security policies by default (only `ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — not present anywhere in this migration — would apply RLS to the owner too). On top of that, `cedar_owner` is the `POSTGRES_USER` from `docker-compose.yml`, which Postgres's init entrypoint creates as a full superuser, and superusers bypass RLS unconditionally regardless of any `FORCE` setting.

report_2.evidence: In PostgreSQL, row-level security policies are not applied to a table's owner unless `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` is also set (it isn't, here), and — more decisively — **superusers always bypass RLS entirely**, regardless of `FORCE`. Since `cedar_owner` is both the owner and a superuser, `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` has no effect on any connection made as `cedar_owner`.

report_1.evidence: Change `cedarline/.env` and `.env.example` so the running application connects as `cedar_app`, e.g. `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`, and leave `cedar_owner` reserved for `scripts/migrate.sh` / one-off admin work only... `cedar_owner` should be usable **only** by `scripts/migrate.sh` and `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` — never referenced by `backend/app/*`. `cedar_app` should be the sole credential the running app and the test suite use

report_2.evidence: Change `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`, and any deploy/staging equivalent, e.g. under `infra/`) so the app's `DATABASE_URL` uses `cedar_app`, not `cedar_owner`... **Migration/schema-owner role** (`cedar_owner`) — used only by `scripts/migrate.sh` / deploy-time migration tooling. Never referenced by the running API process. **Application runtime role** (`cedar_app`) — used exclusively by `backend/app/db.py`'s engine for all request traffic.

report_1.evidence: That bootstrap file is explicitly documented as a manual, one-time step — its own header comment says "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup," and both `README.md` and `docs/local-setup.md` list it as a separate step 2... Since `reset-local-db.sh`... never invokes that bootstrap script, every full reset wipes out the `cedar_app` role along with the rest of the cluster state, and nothing recreates it automatically.

report_2.evidence: `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`... is explicitly documented, in both its own header comment and `README.md`/`docs/local-setup.md`, as a **one-time, manual** step — it lives in `db/bootstrap/`, not `db/migrations/`, and `scripts/migrate.sh` only iterates `db/migrations/*.sql`. `reset-local-db.sh` never calls it... So after any `reset-local-db.sh` run..., `cedar_app` is gone, and any connection attempt using it... fails immediately with Postgres's `FATAL: role "cedar_app" does not exist`.
